data.title: London's FTSE 100 retreats from highs as HSBC weighs on bank stocks
data.description:
Key points:
  • FTSE 100 down 0.4%, FTSE 250 up 0.05%
  • HSBC falls after plans to buy rest of Hang Seng Bank
  • Homebuilders fall after RICS housing data
  • Volution hits record high after annual results

London's FTSE 100 retreated from the previous session's record high on Thursday, as a decline in the shares of HSBC weighed on the banking sector.

The blue-chip FTSE 100 CURRENCYCOM:UK100 fell 0.4%, while the mid-cap-focused FTSE 250 was up 0.05%.

HSBC dropped 5.4%, logging its steepest one-day drop in more than six months. The bank said it plans to buy out minority interests in Hong Kong's Hang Seng Bank, where it holds a majority stake, in a deal worth HK$106.1 billion ($13.63 billion).

The broader banking sector (.FTNMX301010) weighed heavily on the market with a 3.7% decline.

Lloyds Banking Group and Merchant bank Close Brothers dropped 3.3% and 12.8%, respectively, after both the lenders warned they may need to set aside more cash to cover the costs related to a motor finance scandal.

Lloyds bank also announced plans to buy out Schroders' 49.9% stake in their UK wealth management joint venture.

Aston Martin slumped 12.6% and was among the top losers in the FTSE 250, after European peer Ferrari's 15.4% fall on disappointment over the luxury carmaker's new long-term financial targets.

The broader European auto stocks (.SXAP) fell 4.4%, marking their biggest daily fall since March.

The wider homebuilders (.FTNMX402020) index shed 1.9%, with Taylor Wimpey and Barratt Redrow dropping 4.6% and 3.6%, respectively.

A survey published by the Royal Institution of Chartered Surveyors said Britain's housing market lost momentum for a third consecutive month, and its measures of buyer demand and agreed sales were stuck in the negative territory in September.

On the flip side, Volution Group closed at a record high, up 5.2%, after posting strong fiscal year results, helping the construction and materials sector (.FTNMX501010) inch up 0.9%.

The FTSE 100 has been rallying to record highs, on a boost from resource-linked stocks and banks this week. It recorded its best weekly showing since October 2008 on Friday as renewed investor interest in pharma stocks led them to regain some ground lost through the year.

Secure Trust Bank shares dropped 20.2% after the bank said it expects annual underlying profit before tax to fall below market expectations.

data.title: Alternative Labor Data Suggest Not-Hiring-Not-Firing Continues — Market Talk
data.description: The government shutdown starves Wall Street of statistics particularly relevant now that investors look at U.S. labor data to forecast how fast the Fed could cut interest rates. Ameriprise's Russell Price is looking into private-sector data, including the ADP employment report and ISM surveys. "They are not as comprehensive or as deep as the [BLS's] employment report," he says, but they "give you a better sense of the direction" of labor markets. Price also uses alternative estimates for weekly jobless claims. The available indicators suggest employers remain reluctant to both hire and lay off workers, he says. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

data.title: Silver Hits Historic $50 Mark as Investors Rush to Safety — Commodities Roundup
data.description:
MARKET MOVEMENTS:

  • Brent crude oil is down 1% to $65.61 a barrel
  • European benchmark gas is up 0.4% to 32.49 euros a megawatt-hour
  • Gold futures are down 1% to $4,030.20 a troy ounce
  • LME three-month copper futures are up 1.6% to $10,870 a metric ton

TOP STORY:

Silver Hits Historic $50 Mark as Investors Rush to Safety

Spot silver prices reached the historic $50 mark on Thursday, bolstered by gold's record-breaking rally, growing investor demand for safe havens and persistent supply shortfalls.

In afternoon European trade, the precious metal rose 2.6% to $50.16 a troy ounce, a level not seen since 1980, while futures in New York traded at $48.33 an ounce.

Silver has advanced in tandem with gold this year, but at a much faster pace.

OTHER STORIES:

Orsted to Slash 2,000 Jobs in Coming Years

Orsted will cut a quarter of its workforce over the next couple of years as it scales back its global footprint to focus more on offshore wind markets in Europe and select regions in Asia Pacific.

The job cuts come as the Danish renewable-energy company seeks to save costs, while its more focused market strategy and the imminent completion of a number of projects means it needs to streamline.

--

Agnico Eagle Makes Additional C$8.25M Investment in Fuerte Metals

Agnico Eagle Mines bumped up its investment in Fuerte Metals by 8.25 million Canadian dollars ($5.9 million) as the Canadian miner continues to build stakes in projects where it sees geological potential.

The Canadian gold producer said it bought 5 million subscription receipts in a wholly owned subsidiary of Fuerte at C$1.65 apiece.

--

Johnson Matthey Adjusts Guidance Amid Sale of Catalyst Business

Johnson Matthey adjusted its expectations for its fiscal year as a whole as it readies the sale of its catalyst unit.

The British platinum refiner said Thursday that the outlook for the year as a whole is now at the higher end of the initial guidance of mid-single-digit growth in underlying operating profit. This is excluding catalyst technologies, and at constant precious metal prices and currency.

--

G&M: Deep Sky unveils plans for one of the world's biggest direct air carbon capture facilities in Manitoba

Carbon-removal startup Deep Sky Corp. plans to build a $500-million direct-air-capture plant in southwestern Manitoba, one of the world's largest facilities designed to counteract the buildup of CO2 in the atmosphere.

Montreal-based Deep Sky will announce Thursday that it is developing a project that will absorb 500,000 tonnes of carbon dioxide annually, dwarfing its newly operational demonstration plant in central Alberta.

--

Metlen Energy & Metals to Form Greek Battery JV With Karatzis Group of Companies

Metlen Energy & Metals agreed to form a joint venture with Karatzis Group of Companies to develop a 330 megawatt battery energy storage system in Greece.

The companies will own 49% and 51% respectively in the joint venture, which will require a total investment of 170 million euros ($197.7 million). The investment is expected to be completed within the second quarter of 2026.

--

Ecosystem Investment Partners Raises Over $400 Million for Environment-Restoration Bets

Ecosystem Investment Partners has raised $401.3 million for its latest private-equity fund to invest in environmental restoration and conservation projects across the U.S., a strategy that benefits from infrastructure development and the need to reduce its impact.

The fund

Baltimore-based EIP wrapped up the fund, Ecosystem Investment Partners V, with roughly 12% less capital than the $454.5 million it raised for a predecessor vehicle, which closed about five years ago as a private-equity fundraising boom faded. The firm now manages more than $1 billion.

--

Challenger Energy, Sintana to Form Combined Oil-And-Gas Exploration Group

Oil-and-gas firm Challenger Energy Group is to merge with peer Sintana Energy, a move the companies say will diversify their portfolios and boost their scale and shareholder returns.

Under the terms of the agreed deal, Sintana will acquire all shares in AIM-listed Challenger, with shareholders receiving 0.47 new Sintana shares per each share in Challenger. Challenger shareholders will hold around 25% of the combined group's share capital, the companies said. Sintana will meanwhile list on the AIM this quarter, adding to its current listings in Canada and the U.S.

--

China Imposes New Controls Over Rare-Earth Exports

China introduced new controls on rare-earth exports on Thursday, requiring foreign entities to obtain government approval before re-exporting products of Chinese origin to other countries.

Foreign organizations and individuals must now obtain a dual-use export license from China's Ministry of Commerce before exporting rare-earth products, technologies, or other dual-use items to destinations outside China, according to new rules issued by the ministry.

--

Gold Screams 'Debasement Trade.' Bonds Say Otherwise.

Debasement fears seem to be everywhere-except the one place they should be most obvious: bonds. Sure, there was a nudge up in global long-end yields this week, thanks to politics. Japan's ruling party picked a leader who likes big spending and low rates, and France lost yet another prime minister after failing to reconcile the need to save money with a parliament that disagrees.

Yet, the bond markets remain confident that U.S. inflation won't run out of control, even as investors point to soaring gold prices as a sign of fear that politicians and central banks will choose inflation as the easy way out of debt problems.

MARKET TALKS:

Gold Rally Seen Underpinned by Central Banks Ditching Treasurys — Market Talk

1134 ET - Gold's rally takes a break, but prices remains above $4,000 an ounce. Ameriprise's Russell Price attributes the metal's strength to global central banks replacing Treasurys with gold in their reserves. He points to China. "They want to further build the credibility of their currency and their central bank and financial system overall," he says. Price adds that concerns about the U.S. budget deficit undermines demand for long-term Treasurys, although the securities are doing well this year. "I've been really surprised about the performance of the Treasurys," he says. Price suspects that a weaker dollar and falling interest rates in Europe and Japan keep Treasurys from losing value fast. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

--

Hog Futures Extend Their Slide — Market Talk

1130 ET - Lean hog futures on the CME are down 0.5%. If the contract closes lower, it'll be the fourth consecutive day that hog futures have settled in the negative. An elevated slaughter rate is one reason that the contract is under pressure, says StoneX in a note. "Following higher slaughter rates, the cutout printed lower by $2.21 to $103.82 [per hundredweight] as we get into larger slaughter periods." The firm adds that the build in supply comes after the passing of the summer grilling season - although the upcoming holiday season is expected to consume that higher production. Live cattle futures are up 0.4% in morning trade. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

--

U.S. Natural Gas Futures Lower After Storage Data — Market Talk

1046 ET - U.S. natural gas futures are lower after a weekly inventory build lands at the high end of expectations. Gas in underground storage rose by 80 billion cubic feet last week to 3,641 Bcf, and was 157 Bcf above the five-year average for the time of year, the EIA reports. The injection was larger than the average 75 Bcf estimate in a Wall Street Journal survey of analysts, but smaller than the five-year average build for the week of 94 Bcf. Nymex natural gas is down 0.6% at $3.313/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

--

Oil Futures Edge Higher in Rangebound Trade — Market Talk

0932 ET - Crude futures move higher after slipping in earlier trade on a deal aimed at ending the Israel-Hamas war and hostage crisis. Oil futures are "in a corrective phase as the Israel/Hamas conflict looks to be ending," Dennis Kissler of BOK Financial says in a note. The large U.S. crude oil stock build reported yesterday was offset by draws in diesel and gasoline inventories, pointing to buoyant fuel demand, he says. "With rising crude inventories, along with stronger fuel demand, crude looks to be locked in a $60-$65/bbl range in the near term." WTI is up 0.3% at $62.74 a barrel, and Brent is 0.2% higher at $66.38.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

--

Wheat Higher as Russian Seeding Falls — Market Talk

0921 ET - Russia's agricultural minister says the country is expected to seed 2% less wheat this marketing year than it did in the last one, counter to expectations of more wheat being planted. Russian farmers appear to be choosing to plant more oilseeds, says AgResource in a note, adding that farmers are expected to seed 8.5% more oilseed crops including soybeans, due to higher profit margins versus wheat. CBOT wheat futures are up 0.8% premarket, while corn is down 0.1% and soybeans slip 0.6%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

--

EDP Renovaveis to Benefit From U.S. Power-Demand Boom — Market Talk

1227 GMT - EDP Renovaveis is the best-positioned among EU-listed utilities to benefit from the surge in U.S. electricity demand driven by AI and data centers, Jefferies analysts say. The U.S. power-demand boom is a key driver of growth for the Portuguese company, which is now moving from years of earnings downgrades to a phase of consistent growth, Jefferies says in a note. The company derives around 50 to 60% of its earnings from the U.S., where it has an attractive asset mix and supply chain setup, Jefferies says. Furthermore, improved policy clarity from the "One Big Beautiful Bill Act" and treasury guidance are easing earlier worries around growth in the country, Jefferies adds. The bank upgrades the stock to buy from hold. Shares are up 3.1%. (maitane.sardon@wsj.com)

--

Gold Eases on Gaza Deal, Profit-Taking — Market Talk 1115 GMT - Gold prices remain slightly pressured by easing geopolitical risks in the Middle East and some profit-taking, with futures in New York down 0.3% to $4,059 a troy ounce. "Strong ETF inflows, momentum, and FOMO [fear of missing out]-driven buying have pushed prices into technically overbought territory, prompting some short-term caution," analysts at Saxo Bank say. Still, the precious metal remains well supported by a rush to safe-haven assets due to growing political uncertainties, prospects of further U.S. interest rate cuts and central-bank buying. Meanwhile, silver futures trade 0.9% lower to $48.55 an ounce. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

--

Oil Falls With Gaza Deal, U.S. Crude Stocks in Focus — Market Talk

1053 GMT - Oil prices fall in afternoon trade, pressured by easing geopolitical risks in the Middle East and higher U.S. crude inventories. Brent slips 0.5% to $65.89 a barrel, while WTI is down 0.6% to $61.67 a barrel. Israel and Hamas agreed to the first phase of President Trump's peace plan for Gaza, a move that could reduce the region's risk premium. Meanwhile, U.S. crude inventories rose for a second straight week, sending bearish signals about demand in the world's largest oil consumer. Oil prices have fallen around 10% this year, as fears of an impending supply glut continue to weigh on sentiment. Citi analysts say sentiment remains "univocally bearish," with some doubting that a $60-a-barrel Brent floor will be enough to keep the market in balance. Others expect a more moderate price correction, with rising oil inventories outside key hubs expected to prevent sharp price drops. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

--

Palm Oil Gains on Strength in Rival Oils, Expected Inventory Decline — Market Talk

1037 GMT - Palm oil closed higher, supported by overnight gains in rival oils and continued buying momentum after breaching the 4,500-ringgit level, Kenanga Futures writes in a note. Expectations for a decline in Malaysia's September inventories for the first time in seven months, and optimism on Indonesia's progress in testing its 50% palm-based biodiesel blend are further lifting market sentiment. However, upside could be limited by potential profit-taking pressure and traders' caution ahead of Malaysia Palm Oil Board data due Friday, Kenanga adds. It sees support for the December futures contract at 4,500 ringgit a ton and resistance at 4,630 ringgit. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for December delivery rose 49 ringgit to 4,594 ringgit a ton. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

--

Teck Resources' Guidance Cut Shows Production Growth Further Away Than Expected — Market Talk

0946 GMT - Teck Resources' operational update and revised production guidance could stymie calls from its shareholders for an improved offer from Anglo American, Berenberg analysts Richard Hatch and William Dalby write. The two miners have agreed to merge in one of the mining industry's largest ever deals. Teck's guidance cut shows that expected production growth will take longer to achieve than first thought, they write. Anglo American backed Teck's update, saying it is broadly in line with its due diligence and said it has addressed a similar issue to Teck's at Quebrada Blanca, Chile. This is a good start and will help assuage market concerns, they add. Anglo American shares trade up 1.8% at 2,951 pence while Tech's shares closed at 60.6 Canadian dollars Wednesday. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

--

Cocoa Falls on Softening Demand, Improved Weather — Market Talk

0845 GMT - Cocoa prices fall in early trading on signs of weakening global demand and improved weather conditions in West Africa, marking a significant change in trajectory. Futures in New York are down 0.7% to $5,956 a ton after tumbling to a 20-month low in the previous session. "Cocoa bean processing has declined over the last three quarters in Asia, Europe, and North America, and is widely expected to drop further in the upcoming quarterly reports due next week," analysts at ING say. "Meanwhile, good rains have boosted crop development in the Ivory Coast and Ghana, while both nations have raised farmgate prices this season, encouraging farmers to increase deliveries to warehouses." (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

--

European Gas Price Falls on Easing Supply Concerns — Market Talk

0828 GMT - European natural-gas prices fall in early trading, with the benchmark Dutch TTF contract down 1.7% to 32.14 euros a megawatt hour. Storage sites across the EU are at nearly 83% of capacity, according to industry group Gas Infrastructure Europe. "While this is lower than the five-year seasonal average of 91%, it's on schedule to reach its targeted levels before the heating season begins," ANZ analysts say. The region is also benefiting from strong imports of liquefied natural gas, with demand in North Asian markets cooling down due to recent price hikes and high inventories in Japan. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

--

Copper Gains as Teck Resources Production Guidance Cut Raises Supply Concerns — Market Talk

0809 GMT - Copper prices climb in early trading after Canadian mining giant Teck Resources cut its production guidance due to continued challenges at its mine in Chile, raising concerns over global supply. Futures on the London Metal Exchange rise 1.6% to $10,874 a metric ton, reaching their highest level since May 2024. "Copper has surged around 23% so far this year, driven by supply disruptions at major mines that outweigh weak demand in major industrial economies," ING analysts say. Meanwhile, the International Copper Study Group forecasts a supply deficit of 150,000 tons next year from previous estimates of a 209,000-ton surplus. The group also narrowed its 2025 surplus estimates to 178,000 tons from 289,000 tons earlier. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

--

Gold Eases After Gaza Deal But Holds Near Record Highs — Market Talk

0752 GMT - Gold prices fall in early trading as the first phase of a Gaza deal between Israel and Hamas eases geopolitical risks, but continue to hover near record highs. Futures in New York are down 0.5% to $4,051.40 a troy ounce after hitting an all-time high in the previous session. The precious metal continues to trade above the $4,000 historic mark as investors seek safety amid growing economic and political uncertainty. "Concerns over the U.S. economy and the government shutdown continue to boost investor demand," analysts at ANZ Research say. Strong ETF inflows and expectations that the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates further this year are also supporting prices, with major Wall Street banks saying the yellow metal has room to climb even higher. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

--

Oil Little Changed as Investors Focus on Gaza Deal — Market Talk

0737 GMT - Oil prices are little changed in early trading, as investors turn their focus on a long-sought deal in Gaza that could ease geopolitical risks in the Middle East. Brent crude and WTI edge 0.3% higher to $62.19 and $66.43 a barrel, respectively, after opening the session lower. "The immediate impact on the oil markets will be a slight decrease in the geopolitical risk premium--about 1%-2% of the current Brent price--as the markets figure out the details of the peace plan," says Claudio Galimberti, chief economist at Rystad Energy. "In the longer term, if the plan for stable peace proves credible, its impact on prices could be more structural and profound." The repercussions for oil markets could be significant, from diminished disruptions in the Red Sea to renewed prospects for a nuclear deal with Iran and, potentially, a rise in Iranian crude exports, Galimberti says. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

--

London's Miners Lead FTSE 100 as Attention Turns to Copper — Market Talk

0736 GMT - London's miners lead the FTSE 100 in opening trade as investor attention turns to copper. Anglo American is the index's largest gainer and trades up 3.1%. Chilean copper miner Antofagasta rises 2.7%, Rio Tinto's London-listed shares are up 2.2% and Glencore rises 1.2%. Copper may be the next hot metals trade, and there is a growing consensus that it might surge to record highs, research director at XTB Kathleen Brooks says. Gold has stolen the limelight this year, but the rise of AI means it's copper's time to shine, she adds. LME copper falls 0.5% but CME copper rises 1.7% and SHFE copper jumps 4.3%.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

--

China's Latest Rare-Earth Controls to Have Limited Impact on Exports — Market Talk

0451 GMT - China's latest step up in rare-earth controls will likely have limited impact on the actual flow of the material's exports, ING chief China economist Lynn Song says. China introduced new controls on rare-earth exports on Thursday, requiring foreign entities to obtain government approval before re-exporting products of Chinese origin to other countries. Rare earths have been a bargaining chip for China in trade negotiations with the U.S. With a month to go before the truce is set to expire, ING thinks that the two sides could reach another extension. "Closing potential back channels for redirecting rare-earth exports shouldn't materially change these discussions as full-on export controls have always been an available tool to be adjusted as needed," he says. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

--

Iron Ore Rises on Higher Seasonal Demand — Market Talk

0335 GMT - Iron ore rises in early Asian trade, with the contract for December delivery on the Dalian Commodity Exchange rising 1.0% to 800.5 yuan a ton. The industry remained stable during the market closure for China's Golden Week, with shipments steady and steel mills restocking on higher end-user seasonal demand, analysts at Nanhua Futures write in a note. There is a decline in inventories and a slight improvement in fundamentals, they say. The market will likely be concerned with efforts to limit China's steel exports to the EU, after European officials proposed a 50% tariff on steel imports above a certain quota, the ANZ Research team writes in a note. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

--

Chinese Gold Miners Set to Shine on Higher Gold Prices — Market Talk 0212 GMT - China's gold miners stand to benefit from higher gold prices, HSBC Global Investment Research analysts say in a report. Gold prices could continue rising in the short term and into 1H 2026 due to factors such as geopolitical risks and financial-market turbulence, the analysts say. HSBC raises its average gold forecasts to $3,355/oz from $3,215/oz for 2025, to $3,950/oz from $3,125/oz for 2026 and to $3,600/oz from $2,925/oz for 2027. HSBC also raises target prices of Zijin Mining's H shares to HK$41.80 from HK$37.70, Shandong Gold Mining's A shares to CNY50.00 from CNY36.00 and Zhaojin Mining Industry's H shares to HK$37.10 from HK$23.70. It has buy ratings on all three stocks. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

Write to Barcelona Editors at barcelonaeditors@dowjones.com

data.title: GM relaunches affordable Bolt EV
data.description:
General Motors said on Thursday it was bringing its affordable Bolt electric vehicle back, as the Detroit automaker looks to widen its offerings in the space.

The Bolt's launch comes at a time when tariff-induced higher prices and fewer EV-related federal subsidies are pressuring consumers.

GM said the Bolt would launch at a starting price of just under $30,000, with a cheaper variant scheduled to be rolled out later in the year.

The EV would be offered with Tesla's North American Charging Standard and is expected to have a range of 255 miles, the company said.

Notice is also hereby given that the Issuer and the Trustee have agreed to the above waivers as of 9 October 2025 and such waivers became effective on the delivery of the trustee consent letter dated 9 October 2025 and that the Vendor MSA has been entered into on 9 October 2025.

Terms used but not defined in this notice bear the same meaning as set out in the incorporated terms memorandum entered into between, inter alios, Temple Quay No.1 PLC and the Trustee dated 15 November 2022.

Notes:

1.

Rule 8 of the Takeover Code (the "Code") sets out the circumstances in which Dealing Disclosures and/or Opening Position Disclosures are required to be made by the parties to an offer, persons acting in concert with them, persons with an interest in relevant securities of 1% or more and exempt principal traders ("EPTs") connected with the parties to an offer.

2.

Where an offeror is marked "Disclosure of dealings and positions in this offeror is not required", dealings and positions in relevant securities of that offeror are not required to be disclosed under Rule 8. This is either because it has been announced that the offer or possible offer is, or is likely to be, solely in cash or because disclosure is not required for some other reason.

3.

Dealings and positions required to be disclosed under Rule 8 of the Code include dealings and positions in derivatives referenced to, and options in respect of, relevant securities of an offeree company and, where appropriate, an offeror (see Note 2 above).

4.

The information set out in the Disclosure Table relating to the relevant securities in issue of an offeree company or of an offeror (and any amendments thereto) has been extracted from the relevant announcement made by such company under Rule 2.9 of the Code. Similarly, details of any deadline set under Rule 2.6 by which a potential offeror must announce either a firm intention to make a bid or that it does not intend to make an offer (or, in the case of Rule 2.6(a), by which the Panel must consent to an extension of the deadline) has been extracted from the relevant announcement made by the offeree company.

5.

Additions and deletions of offeree companies and offerors are reflected in the Disclosure Table throughout the day, following the relevant announcement. Other amendments may also be made throughout the day. In any event, details of the classes of relevant securities issued by an offeree or offeror company, the number of such securities in issue ("NSI") and the International Securities Identification Number ("ISIN") of such securities announced under Rule 2.9 of the Code before 5.00 pm each day will be included in the final version of the Disclosure Table by 5.30 pm that day. The final version of the Disclosure Table issued each day will be marked "Final" and persons required to make Dealing Disclosures and Opening Position Disclosures are advised to use that final version. Any changes to the Rule 2.6 deadline of a potential offeror will also be reflected in the "Final" version of the Disclosure Table, although any such changes will not be shown in the "Amendments" section at the top of the Disclosure Table.

6.

Dealing Disclosures by the parties to an offer, persons acting in concert with them and connected EPTs must be made by no later than 12 noon on the business day (as defined in the Code) following the date of the relevant dealing and Dealing Disclosures by persons with an interest in relevant securities of 1% or more must be made by 3.30 pm on the business day following the date of the relevant dealing.

7.

Opening Position Disclosures by the parties to an offer and by connected EPTs must be made by no later than 12 noon on the 10th business day after the commencement of the offer period or the announcement that first identifies an offeror (as the case may be). Where required, Opening Position Disclosures by persons with interests in relevant securities of 1% or more must be made by no later than 3.30 pm on the 10th business day after the commencement of the offer period or the announcement that first identifies an offeror (as the case may be). Details of the time and date at which an offer period commenced and at which an offeror was first identified are set out in the Disclosure Table. In order to calculate the deadline for Opening Position Disclosures, it is therefore necessary to count forward 10 business days from the relevant date.

8.

Opening Position Disclosures and Dealing Disclosures required under Rule 8 of the Code must be made to a Regulatory Information Service ("RIS").

9.

This Note is applicable if it says "See Note 9 below" by the name of the company in the Disclosure Table above.  The Executive has granted the company a dispensation from the requirements under the Code that announcements must be published via a RIS. The company is instead required to publish all announcements on its website. The Executive has also granted a dispensation from Note 3 on Rule 8 of the Code that disclosures made under Rule 8 of the Code must be made to a RIS. Therefore, Opening Position Disclosures and Dealing Disclosures required under Rule 8 of the Code may be made to the company at the address stated in the announcement that commenced the offer period.
